paragraphs.text: The ligand is resolved in SRD-46 as **ligand_6277, Ethylenedinitrilotetraacetic acid (H4L)**, i.e. EDTA. A key sign is the breadth of reported metal complexes: the EDTA stability search returned many metal–EDTA systems, including **Al³⁺, Fe³⁺, Y³⁺, lanthanides, Zr⁴⁺, Hf⁴⁺, Th⁴⁺, and VO²⁺**.

paragraphs.text: At **low pH**, EDTA is more protonated and less available to bind metals strongly. At **near-neutral to alkaline pH**, more deprotonated forms are present, so chelation is much more effective. Chemically, that is why EDTA is excellent as a **broad-spectrum chelator**, but not “universally best” under every condition.

claims.text: At **low pH**, EDTA is more protonated
less available to bind metals strongly
At **near-neutral to alkaline pH**, more deprotonated forms are present
so chelation is much more effective.
EDTA is excellent as a **broad-spectrum chelator**

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: EDTA pKa entries: -1.5 (H4L→H5L), 2 (H3L→H4L), 2.69 (H2L→H3L), 6.13 (HL→H2L) at 25 °C, I=0.1 — lower pH favors more protonated HxL forms.

grounded_claims.evidence_snippet: EDTA protonation at low pH (H4L→H5L pKa -1.5) and fewer bindings below pKa (e.g., L→HL 9.52: M_tot_7 vs 70 above) show it isn’t best under all conditions.

paragraphs.text: One useful pattern from the SRD-46 results is that EDTA shows especially strong affinity for **higher-charge metal ions** such as **Fe³⁺, Al³⁺, Zr⁴⁺, Hf⁴⁺, and Th⁴⁺**, which is exactly what you would expect for a multicarboxylate aminopolycarboxylate ligand.

claims.text: EDTA shows especially strong affinity for **higher-charge metal ions**
Fe³⁺, Al³⁺, Zr⁴⁺, Hf⁴⁺, and Th⁴⁺
which is exactly what you would expect
